name: Actor
summary: A performer who portrays characters in theater, film, television, and radio—one of the oldest paid entertainment roles, from Greek chorus members to screen stars.
duties: Memorize lines, rehearse blocking, perform for audiences or cameras, collaborate with directors, and maintain vocal and physical readiness.
skills: Emotional range, memorization, improvisation, stamina, professionalism on set or stage.
tools: Scripts, costumes, microphones, marks on set, dialect coaches.
regions: Theater districts worldwide; Hollywood, Bollywood, West End, and regional repertory traditions.
originLabel: Classical Greek tragic and comic actors
educationPath: Drama school, conservatory training, audition circuits, on-set experience.
modernSectors: Film, television, theater, voice acting, commercial work, motion capture.

events.label: Cinema acting
events.description: Motion pictures create a new mass market for screen performers.

events.label: Method influence
events.description: Stanislavski-derived training reshapes film and stage acting pedagogy.

name: Playwright
summary: A writer who crafts scripts for live theater—structuring dialogue, conflict, and stage directions for actors and directors.
duties: Draft plays, revise from readings, collaborate in workshops, and negotiate rights with producers.
skills: Dialogue, dramatic structure, character psychology, revision discipline, networking.
tools: Notebooks, typewriters historically, Final Draft and similar software today.
regions: Athens, London, Paris, Broadway, and national theater movements worldwide.
originLabel: Athenian dramatic competitions
educationPath: MFA playwriting programs, fringe festival production, dramaturg apprenticeships.
modernSectors: Regional theater, Broadway, West End, community stages, streaming audio drama.

events.label: Elizabethan theater boom
events.description: Commercial playhouses employ resident playwrights like Shakespeare's circle.

name: Dancer
summary: A performer who expresses stories and rhythm through choreographed movement—spanning court ballets, folk traditions, and commercial music videos.
duties: Rehearse choreography, maintain fitness, perform in ensembles or solos, and adapt to stage or camera.
skills: Technique, musicality, flexibility, memorization, injury prevention.
tools: Dance shoes, studio mirrors, sprung floors, costumes, tour buses.
regions: Every culture with ritual and social dance; professional companies in Europe, Americas, and Asia.
originLabel: Ritual and court dance in early civilizations
educationPath: Conservatory training, company auditions, commercial dance workshops.
modernSectors: Ballet companies, touring pop acts, cruise ships, film choreography, teaching studios.

events.label: Royal academies
events.description: Louis XIV-era institutions formalize professional ballet training in France.

name: Ballet Dancer
summary: A specialist in classical ballet technique who performs pointe work, pas de deux, and repertoire from Petipa to contemporary choreographers.
duties: Daily class, rehearse repertoire, tour with companies, and maintain extraordinary physical conditioning.
skills: Turnout, pointe safety, partnering, musical precision, silent storytelling.
tools: Pointe shoes, tutus, rosin, rehearsal pianists, company studios.
regions: Paris Opera Ballet, Bolshoi, Royal Ballet, American Ballet Theatre, and national companies.
originLabel: Académie Royale de Danse in France
educationPath: Pre-professional academies, company school pipelines, summer intensives.
modernSectors: Major ballet companies, contemporary ballet hybrids, commercial ballet events.

events.label: Romantic ballet
events.description: La Sylphide era elevates pointe technique and ethereal repertoire.

name: Musician
summary: A performer who plays instruments or sings for audiences—encompassing court minstrels, orchestra members, session players, and touring artists.
duties: Practice repertoire, rehearse with ensembles, perform concerts or sessions, and maintain instruments.
skills: Rhythm, ear training, sight-reading, stage presence, collaboration.
tools: Instruments, sheet music, amplifiers, in-ear monitors, recording booths.
regions: Global; patronage courts historically, concert halls and clubs today.
originLabel: Professional musicians in ancient courts and temples
educationPath: Conservatory, private lessons, band circuits, music school degrees.
modernSectors: Orchestras, touring bands, studio session work, weddings, teaching.

events.label: Recorded music industry
events.description: Phonograph and radio stars create new employment beyond live performance alone.

name: Composer
summary: A creator of music who writes scores for concert hall, theater, film, and games—evolving from court kapellmeisters to freelance media composers.
duties: Write notation or DAW projects, orchestrate, revise to director notes, and deliver parts to performers.
skills: Harmony, orchestration, deadline work, collaboration, stylistic range.
tools: Manuscript paper, piano, Sibelius/Finale, DAWs, sample libraries.
regions: Vienna classical tradition, Hollywood scoring stages, game audio studios globally.
originLabel: Notated composition in medieval and Renaissance chapels
educationPath: Composition degrees, mentorship, portfolio of concert and media work.
modernSectors: Symphony commissions, film/TV scoring, video games, advertising, theater.

events.label: Concert tradition
events.description: Public concert halls expand commissions for symphonic composers.

name: Conductor
summary: A leader who interprets scores and directs orchestras, choirs, or pit bands—emerging as a distinct role in the 19th-century concert hall.
duties: Rehearse ensembles, set tempi and dynamics, cue entries, and represent the orchestra publicly.
skills: Score study, baton technique, leadership, interpersonal diplomacy, stamina.
tools: Batons, scores, recording sessions, concert podiums.
regions: European and American symphony institutions; opera houses worldwide.
originLabel: Public conductor-as-star in Romantic orchestra culture
educationPath: Conservatory conducting programs, assistant conductor posts, competitions.
modernSectors: Symphonies, opera pits, youth orchestras, recording labels.

events.label: Recorded conductors
events.description: Early recordings spread celebrity conductor interpretations globally.

name: Opera Singer
summary: A classically trained vocalist who performs staged works with orchestra—combining musicianship, acting, and often multilingual diction.
duties: Learn roles, attend musical and stage rehearsals, perform long scores, and protect vocal health.
skills: Bel canto or equivalent technique, language diction, stamina, stage movement.
tools: Scores, costumes, wigs, resonant practice rooms, vocal coaches.
regions: Italian opera houses, German Festspiel traditions, Met Opera, global festivals.
originLabel: Florentine camerata and early opera around 1600
educationPath: Conservatory voice programs, young artist programs, coachings.
modernSectors: Opera companies, oratorio concerts, crossover recording, teaching.

events.label: Monteverdi Orfeo
events.description: Early masterwork helps establish opera as enduring professional repertoire.

name: Music Producer
summary: A studio professional who shapes recordings by guiding performance, arrangement, and mix decisions—central to pop, hip-hop, and electronic genres.
duties: Select takes, coach artists, hire session players, oversee mixing and mastering budgets.
skills: Ear for hooks, arrangement, diplomacy, technical literacy, trend awareness.
tools: DAWs, control surfaces, outboard gear, reference monitors, sample packs.
regions: Nashville, Los Angeles, London, Atlanta, Seoul pop factories.
originLabel: Independent producers in post-war recording studios
educationPath: Engineering apprenticeships, home studio portfolios, label production deals.
modernSectors: Major labels, indie artists, sync licensing, beat leasing markets.

events.label: MIDI and home studios
events.description: Digital tools democratize production outside expensive commercial studios.

name: Luthier
summary: A craftsperson who builds and repairs stringed instruments—violins, guitars, and related wooden bodies requiring acute acoustic judgment.
duties: Carve tops and backs, fit necks, varnish instruments, setup action, and restore antiques.
skills: Woodworking, acoustics intuition, patience, client consultation, repair diagnosis.
tools: Chisels, bending iron, purfling tools, calipers, humidity-controlled workshop.
regions: Cremona violin tradition, Spanish guitar makers, Nashville repair shops.
originLabel: European plucked and bowed instrument workshops

events.label: Stradivari legend
events.description: Cremonese makers set enduring standards for violin craft prestige.

name: Painter
summary: An artist who applies pigment to surfaces to create images—from temple fresco painters to gallery exhibitors and commercial illustrators.
duties: Prepare supports, mix color, compose images, exhibit work, and fulfill commissions.
skills: Color theory, composition, medium handling, critique resilience, business for freelancers.
tools: Brushes, palettes, easels, canvases, pigments, studio lighting.
regions: Italian Renaissance workshops, Dutch Golden Age, Paris salons, global contemporary scenes.
originLabel: Cave painting traditions
educationPath: Art school, atelier study, residency programs, gallery representation.
modernSectors: Fine art markets, murals, illustration, scenic painting, teaching.

events.label: Renaissance workshops
events.description: Patronage systems employ painters in organized botteghe across Italy.

name: Sculptor
summary: An artist who shapes three-dimensional forms in stone, metal, wood, or clay—from temple carvers to public monument makers.
duties: Model maquettes, carve or cast works, install large pieces, and collaborate with foundries.
skills: Spatial reasoning, material knowledge, physical endurance, engineering liaison for installs.
tools: Chisels, rasps, armatures, kilns, welding gear, CNC aids in modern studios.
regions: Classical Mediterranean, Indian temple ateliers, modern public art programs.
originLabel: Monumental stone carving in early states
educationPath: Fine art degrees, foundry apprenticeships, public art RFQ experience.
modernSectors: Galleries, civic monuments, film props, garden design, restoration.

events.label: Modernist sculpture
events.description: Rodin-era traditions expand abstract and installation practice.

name: Illustrator
summary: A commercial artist who draws images for books, magazines, advertising, and games—distinct from purely gallery-focused painters.
duties: Interpret briefs, sketch concepts, finalize art to print specs, and meet publication deadlines.
skills: Drawing, storytelling, client communication, print and digital file prep.
tools: Ink, tablets, Procreate, Photoshop, style guides from art directors.
regions: Publishing centers in London, New York, Tokyo; freelance global markets online.
originLabel: Mass illustrated periodicals and books
educationPath: Illustration programs, agency portfolios, social media audience building.
modernSectors: Children's books, editorial, advertising, tabletop games, concept art.

events.label: Golden age illustration
events.description: Halftone printing employs household-name magazine illustrators.

name: Poet
summary: A writer who composes verse—oral epic singers, court laureates, and modern slam and lyric poets.
duties: Write poems, perform readings, submit to journals, teach workshops, and compile collections.
skills: Language music, metaphor, revision, performance voice, literary community building.
tools: Notebooks, chapbooks, open mic stages, Substack, recording apps.
regions: Every literate culture; strong public traditions in Ireland, Persia, and contemporary slam scenes.
originLabel: Epic and hymn traditions in oral societies
educationPath: MFA creative writing, self-publishing, festival circuits.
modernSectors: Publishing, academia, spoken word, songwriting overlap, grants.

events.label: Modernist anthologies
events.description: Little magazines and modernist presses expand professional poetry publication paths.

name: Novelist
summary: A writer of long-form fiction who builds characters and worlds across book-length narratives—flourishing after print markets matured.
duties: Draft manuscripts, revise with editors, promote releases, and manage series continuity.
skills: Plot architecture, voice, discipline, research, audience awareness.
tools: Word processors, research archives, agents, publishers, audiobook studios.
regions: London, Paris, New York, Lagos, Tokyo publishing ecosystems.
originLabel: Rise of prose fiction markets in Europe
educationPath: Writing practice, MFAs optional, agent representation, self-publishing platforms.
modernSectors: Trade publishing, genre imprints, audiobooks, adaptation rights.

events.label: Serialized novels
events.description: Magazine serialization pays novelists by installment in Victorian markets.

name: Screenwriter
summary: A writer who crafts scripts for film and television—structuring scenes for camera coverage and production budgets.
duties: Write treatments and drafts, pitch studios, revise to producer notes, and support writers' room sessions.
skills: Visual writing, pacing, dialogue, collaboration, understanding of production constraints.
tools: Screenwriting software, pitch decks, writers' room whiteboards, zoom writers' rooms.
regions: Hollywood, Seoul drama factories, Mumbai, London, streaming hub cities.
originLabel: Silent film scenario writers
educationPath: Film school writing tracks, fellowships, spec script sales, room staffing.
modernSectors: Studios, streamers, advertising, video games, limited series.

events.label: New Hollywood
events.description: Director-writer auteur culture elevates screenplay prestige in US cinema.

name: Cinematographer
summary: A director of photography who designs lighting and camera work to tell stories on screen—successor to early camera operators in silent film.
duties: Plan shots with directors, choose lenses and lights, supervise camera crew, and grade look with colorists.
skills: Exposure, composition, color, leadership on set, equipment knowledge.
tools: Cameras, lenses, dollies, LED panels, light meters, LUT monitors.
regions: Global film production hubs; ASC and equivalents as craft guilds.
originLabel: Camera department specialization in early cinema
educationPath: Film school cinematography, camera PA ladder, reel building.
modernSectors: Features, commercials, music videos, streaming series, documentaries.

events.label: Sound film
events.description: Microphone constraints reshape lighting and camera movement craft.

name: Film Editor
summary: A post-production craftsperson who assembles footage into coherent scenes and stories—often where final narrative rhythm is discovered.
duties: Sync dailies, build assemblies, cut to director notes, manage VFX turnovers, and deliver masters.
skills: Pacing, storytelling, software fluency, diplomacy in review sessions, organization.
tools: Avid, Premiere, DaVinci Resolve, screening rooms, asset servers.
regions: Hollywood post houses, Soho London, Bollywood, global streamer pipelines.
originLabel: Specialized negative cutters and editors in silent-to-sound era
educationPath: Assistant editing tracks, film school post programs, union apprenticeships.
modernSectors: Features, TV rooms, trailers, documentaries, social content.

events.label: Nonlinear editing experiments
events.description: Digital tools eventually replace flatbed-only workflows in most markets.

name: Costume Designer
summary: A designer who creates clothing for characters in theater, film, and television—balancing history, symbolism, and practical quick changes.
duties: Research eras, sketch designs, supervise fittings, manage wardrobe teams, and maintain continuity.
skills: Fashion history, sewing literacy, collaboration, budget tracking, distressing techniques.
tools: Sewing rooms, dye pots, reference libraries, fitting mirrors, continuity Polaroids.
regions: Broadway wardrobe shops, Hollywood costume houses, opera companies.
originLabel: Professional costume departments in film studios
educationPath: Theater design MFAs, wardrobe internships, portfolio of period work.
modernSectors: Film, TV, theater, ballet, cosplay-adjacent commercial work.

events.label: Technicolor prestige
events.description: Golden age studios expand costume shop employment for spectacle films.

name: Set Designer
summary: A production designer or set specialist who builds the visual environment for stage and screen—bridging architecture, art, and storytelling.
duties: Draft set plans, choose materials, coordinate with construction crews, and align with director vision.
skills: Drafting, spatial design, history research, budgeting, safety codes.
tools: Drafting tables, CAD, paint shops, foam carving, scenic flats.
regions: West End and Broadway shops, Hollywood art departments, opera houses.
originLabel: Scenic design in proscenium theater and early film
educationPath: Theater design programs, art department PA tracks, portfolio of builds.
modernSectors: Film, television, theater, events, immersive experiences.

events.label: Widescreen spectacle
events.description: Epic films expand large-scale scenic construction departments.

name: Stage Manager
summary: The operational captain of a live performance who calls cues, coordinates departments, and keeps shows running on time night after night.
duties: Run rehearsals, maintain prompt scripts, call light and sound cues, manage understudies, and enforce safety.
skills: Organization, calm under pressure, communication, diplomacy, precision timing.
tools: Prompt book, headsets, spike tape, show reports, call sheets.
regions: Professional theater worldwide; union stage management in US and UK.
originLabel: Complex touring productions and proscenium stage systems
educationPath: Theater production degrees, ASM apprenticeships, union membership paths.
modernSectors: Broadway, touring musicals, opera, ballet, corporate live events.

events.label: Union stage crews
events.description: Professional associations codify stage management roles and pay scales.

name: Circus Performer
summary: An entertainer skilled in acrobatics, aerial work, clowning, or daredevil acts under tent or arena lights—descended from traveling European circuses.
duties: Train routines, maintain safety rigging awareness, perform multiple shows weekly, and tour with companies.
skills: Athleticism, showmanship, risk management, adaptability, ensemble trust.
tools: Trapeze rigs, tightropes, clown props, crash mats, touring trucks.
regions: Ringling-era America, Cirque du Soleil, Moscow circus schools, Mexican carpas.
originLabel: Modern circus founded by Philip Astley tradition
educationPath: Circus schools, gymnastic backgrounds, company audition circuits.
modernSectors: Big tops, arena spectacles, cruise ships, street busking festivals.

events.label: New circus aesthetics
events.description: Cirque du Soleil reframes circus as arts-first rather than menagerie spectacle.

name: Magician
summary: A performer who presents illusions and sleight-of-hand—entertaining courts, vaudeville halls, and television audiences with crafted misdirection.
duties: Rehearse routines, maintain apparatus, perform shows, and develop new effects.
skills: Manual dexterity, patter, misdirection, audience psychology, secret keeping.
tools: Decks, cups and balls, trap tables, smoke, mirrors, escape apparatus.
regions: European court magic, Las Vegas showrooms, street magic worldwide.
originLabel: Renaissance conjuring pamphlets and court entertainers

events.label: Sala's magic theater
events.description: Dedicated magic theaters popularize stage illusion as a profession in Paris and London.

name: Puppeteer
summary: A performer who animates puppets for theater, film, and children's television—preserving traditions from Indonesian wayang to Muppet-style character work.
duties: Manipulate puppets, voice characters, maintain puppets, and coordinate with directors and camera.
skills: Hand dexterity, voice acting, timing, teamwork in bunraku-style ensembles.
tools: Marionettes, rod puppets, foam characters, monitor feedback for TV.
regions: Java wayang kulit, Japanese bunraku, European puppet theaters, US children's TV.
originLabel: Ritual and folk puppet traditions

events.label: Television puppetry
events.description: Children's TV expands employment for character puppeteers globally.

name: Voice Actor
summary: A performer who supplies voices for animation, games, dubbing, and audiobooks—specialized acting without on-camera presence.
duties: Record lines to picture, match lip flaps in dubbing, create character voices, and attend multiple sessions.
skills: Vocal range, cold reading, mic technique, endurance, direction responsiveness.
tools: Studio booths, pop filters, Pro Tools sessions, scripts, monitors for ADR.
regions: Los Angeles animation hubs, Tokyo seiyū industry, London games audio.
originLabel: Sound cartoon voice performances
educationPath: Voice coaching, demo reels, agency representation, game/animation auditions.
modernSectors: Animation, AAA games, dubbing, audiobooks, advertising VO.

events.label: Games voice expansion
events.description: CD-ROM and later AAA titles increase voiced character workloads.

name: Stand-Up Comedian
summary: A solo performer who tells jokes and observational stories directly to live audiences—modern club circuits grew from vaudeville and coffeehouse scenes.
duties: Write material, test jokes in open mics, tour clubs, record specials, and manage personal brand.
skills: Timing, crowd reading, originality, resilience, brevity editing.
tools: Microphone, notebook, comedy clubs, streaming special platforms.
regions: US club circuit, UK alternative comedy, Montreal festival scene, global Netflix specials.
originLabel: Nightclub comedy in post-war US cities
educationPath: Open mic ladder, writing groups, festival showcases, agent/booker relationships.
modernSectors: Clubs, festivals, streaming specials, podcasting, writing for TV rooms.

events.label: Comedy club boom
events.description: Dedicated comedy clubs create full-time touring circuits in major US cities.

name: Talent Agent
summary: A representative who negotiates contracts and bookings for performers—institutionalized as Hollywood and theater agencies matured.
duties: Pitch clients, negotiate deals, coordinate auditions, and manage long-term career strategy with managers.
skills: Negotiation, relationship networks, contract literacy, taste, hustle.
tools: Breakdown services, contracts, CRM, phones, festival party circuits.
regions: Los Angeles, New York, London, Seoul talent agencies.
originLabel: Hollywood agency system consolidation
educationPath: Mailroom-to-agent tracks at agencies, entertainment law overlap, internships.
modernSectors: Film/TV agencies, music booking, sports crossover agencies, voiceover reps.

events.label: Television expansion
events.description: TV demand multiplies scripted roles and agent representation for working actors.

name: Impresario
summary: A show producer and financier who organized opera, concert, and theater seasons—blending artistic taste with entrepreneurial risk before modern arts administration.
duties: Hire companies, rent halls, market subscriptions, balance budgets, and curate repertoires.
skills: Taste-making, fundraising, logistics, patron relations, risk tolerance.
tools: Contracts, season brochures, box office ledgers, patron lists.
regions: Italian opera impresarios, London concert promoters, 18th–19th century Europe.
originLabel: Commercial opera and concert promotion

events.label: Permanent institutions
events.description: Founded opera houses and symphony boards replace many individual impresario risk models.

name: Muralist
summary: A painter who works at architectural scale on walls and public spaces—from Mesoamerican temples to New Deal fresco programs and street art walls.
duties: Design compositions, prepare surfaces, scaffold safely, apply durable pigments, and engage communities.
skills: Large-scale composition, scaffolding safety, public art process, color permanence knowledge.
tools: Scaffolds, spray equipment, acrylics, lifts, community workshop materials.
regions: Mexico muralism, Italian fresco churches, US WPA walls, global street art festivals.
originLabel: Monumental wall painting in classical and indigenous traditions
educationPath: Fine art training, public art RFQs, apprenticeship with experienced muralists.
modernSectors: Civic percent-for-art programs, corporate lobbies, festivals, community nonprofits.

events.label: WPA murals
events.description: US federal arts projects employ muralists in public buildings nationwide.

name: Tattoo Artist
summary: A body artist who applies permanent pigment designs to skin—evolving from sailor and carnival traditions into licensed studio professions.
duties: Consult clients, draw stencils, tattoo safely, maintain sterile equipment, and manage shop bookings.
skills: Drawing, color packing, line work, hygiene protocols, client rapport.
tools: Tattoo machines, inks, autoclaves, stencils, studio chairs.
regions: Pacific Island traditions, Japanese irezumi, Western sailor ports, global studio culture.
originLabel: Electric tattoo machine patent era
educationPath: Apprenticeships, bloodborne pathogen certification, portfolio building.
modernSectors: Street shops, fine-art tattoo studios, convention circuits, cosmetic tattooing.

events.label: Studio professionalization
events.description: Health regulations and art-school graduates elevate tattoo shop standards in US cities.

name: Calligrapher
summary: A lettering artist who writes beautiful hand-formed scripts for manuscripts, signage, and ceremonies—central to East Asian literati culture and European monastic scriptoria.
duties: Practice scripts, layout compositions, write commissions, and teach workshops.
skills: Stroke control, rhythm, ink consistency, layout design, patience.
tools: Brushes, nibs, ink sticks, rice paper, guidelines, light tables.
regions: China and Japan traditions, Islamic manuscript arts, Western formal hands.
originLabel: Early literate scribal arts

events.label: Print reduces manuscript demand
events.description: Movable type shifts calligraphy toward art and ceremony rather than bulk copying in Europe.

name: Choreographer
summary: A dance maker who designs movement for stage, screen, and music videos—distinct from performers who execute steps created by others.
duties: Create phrases, teach dancers, rehearse spacing, and align movement with music and narrative.
skills: Spatial imagination, teaching clarity, collaboration with composers and directors, notation literacy.
tools: Studio mirrors, video playback, dance notation systems, rehearsal schedules.
regions: Ballet masters historically; Broadway, K-pop, and contemporary companies today.
originLabel: Court ballet masters in Baroque Europe
educationPath: Dancer background, choreographic fellowships, commission portfolios.
modernSectors: Ballet companies, musical theater, pop tours, film, competitive dance TV.

events.label: Modern dance pioneers
events.description: Graham and Duncan traditions assert choreographer as auteur separate from ballet hierarchy.
